preamble: The shift from a voiceless alveolar stop [t] to a voiceless labiodental fricative [f] typically proceeds via an intermediate dental or labiodental fricative stage. The most common pathway is spirantization of [t] to [θ] followed by place-of-articulation shift to [f] (TH-fronting), but direct stop-to-fricative shifts with place change are also documented in aspirate-to-fricative development. The change combines lenition (stop weakening) with a labial place shift, making it typologically marked but attested in several language families.

examples.note: TH-fronting: /θ/ → /f/ in many dialects including Cockney, Bristol, Estuary English, AAVE, and Liberian English. Historically, English /θ/ derives from PIE *t via Grimm's Law, so the chain t > θ > f is completed across historical stages. 'Three' (< PIE *trih₂), 'thin', 'thought' all show the full t→θ→f pathway when dialect forms are compared diachronically.
Attested in Cockney and Estuary English. Diachronically: PIE *treyés > Proto-Germanic *þrīz [θ] > Modern English [θ] > dialectal [f].

examples.from: Standard Bengali ph [pʰ] → [f]; Standard Bengali th [tʰ] → [f] in some varieties
examples.note: In eastern Bengali dialects (Sylheti, Chittagong), the aspirated dental stop [tʰ] (written থ tha) has developed toward [f] in some speakers, parallel to the well-documented pʰ→f shift. This aspirate-to-fricative shift (debuccalization of aspiration) is distinct from simple spirantization: the [h]-like aspiration component merges with the labial position to produce [f].

examples.from: Ancient Greek Θ [tʰ] (theta, aspirated dental stop)
examples.to: Modern Greek θ [θ], and eventually [f] in some borrowings
examples.note: Ancient Greek theta was an aspirated stop [tʰ]. It first shifted to [θ] in Koine Greek, and in clusters (e.g., -νθ- → /nf/ in Modern Greek) the dental fricative further shifted to /f/. E.g., Greek ἄνθρωπος ánthrōpos > Modern Greek άνθρωπος pronounced [ˈanθropos] with the cluster /nθ/ realized as [nf] in some Greek dialects and Italian loanword adaptations.

In Tok Pisin and other English-based creoles, English [θ] is variably adapted as [t], [f], or [d]. The t~f variation directly reflects the perceptual overlap between the dental stop and labiodental fricative across English varieties, and the t→f form is widespread in areas where speakers lack English /θ/.

sources: Wells, J.C. (1982). Accents of English. Cambridge University Press.
Kouwenberg, S. & Singler, J.V. (eds.) (2008). The Handbook of Pidgin and Creole Studies. Wiley-Blackwell.
Horrocks, G. (2010). Greek: A History of the Language and its Speakers. Wiley-Blackwell.

related.label: Intermediate step: t → θ (Grimm's Law, spirantization)
Parallel: p → f (Grimm's Law, Arabic, etc.)
